aliases: Din Tai Fung Hot and Sour Soup

english: Din Tai Fung Hot and Sour Soup

region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Northern Chinese tradition); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Chinese soups — hot and sour style

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 covers hot and sour soup generically; the spicy variant with tofu, mushroom, bamboo shoot, and black fungus is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers hot and sour soup as a category. The specific spicy Din Tai Fung restaurant variant is not individually coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for hot and sour soup as a food item. SNOMED CT is not applicable here.

preparation_method: seasoned with vinegar and white pepper

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular serving of spicy hot and sour soup is approximately 350 g, equivalent to a standard restaurant bowl as served at Din Tai Fung Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and Sour Soup — nutrient profile estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for hot and sour soup (NDB 06416) and component ingredients (tofu, mushroom, bamboo shoot, black fungus, egg, vinegar, starch), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo data for Chinese soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for hot and sour soup (NDB 06416, canned condensed, prepared) was used as the primary anchor. Values were adapted upward for sodium and adjusted for the restaurant-style spicy variant with additional chilli and vinegar. HPB Singapore Foodo Chinese soup category data was used to cross-check energy and protein ranges. The spicy Din Tai Fung variant is not individually listed in any approved primary source; values represent a best-supported estimate for a restaurant-prepared hot and sour soup of this composition.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous mixed vegetable and tofu soup; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; University of Sydney GI Database — mixed vegetable soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for spicy hot and sour soup. GI of 35 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed vegetable soups with starch thickener and tofu (GI range 30–40, low category). The starch thickener (cornstarch) raises GI slightly above plain vegetable soup but the overall GI remains low due to the high water content, vinegar (which slows gastric emptying), and low carbohydrate density. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 14.7) / 100 = 5.1 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1330 mg per regular serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this soup, as a single regular serving provides approximately 1330 mg sodium — more than half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-sodium preparation or consume only a small portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~5.1) due to high water content, vinegar, and modest carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spicy hot and sour soup has a low glycemic index and low glycemic load, making it a relatively suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high sodium content warrants caution and portion control is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium per serving combined with moderate cholesterol from egg; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1330 mg per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low and the soup contains beneficial plant-based ingredients (mushrooms, bamboo shoots, black fungus), but individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Hot and Sour Soup (Regular) — known in Chinese as 辣味酸辣汤 (là wèi suān là tāng) — is a classic Chinese soup dish characterised by its bold balance of sour, spicy, and savoury flavours combined with a smooth, silky, starch-thickened texture. The soup is prepared by simmering a flavourful stock with hand-sliced mushrooms, bean curd strips (tofu), tender bamboo shoots, and black fungus (wood ear mushroom), then seasoning with rice vinegar or black vinegar for sourness, white pepper and chilli for heat, and finishing with a starch slurry to achieve its signature velvety consistency. An egg drop is typically swirled in at the end to add richness and body. The version served at Din Tai Fung in Singapore represents a refined, restaurant-quality interpretation of this traditional dish. Nutritionally, the soup is relatively low in calories (~38 kcal per 100 g), low in fat (~1.4 g per 100 g), and provides modest protein (~2.8 g per 100 g) from tofu and egg. Its primary nutritional concern is sodium, with a regular serving (~350 g) delivering approximately 1330 mg — a significant proportion of the daily recommended intake. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though sodium remains a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. The vinegar content may additionally slow gastric emptying, further moderating postprandial glucose response. This soup is a popular starter or accompaniment in Chinese restaurant dining across Singapore and is widely recognised across Chinese-speaking communities in Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: The spicy hot and sour soup as served at Din Tai Fung Singapore is not individually listed in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy hot and sour soup data (NDB 06416) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Chinese soup category ranges. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed vegetable soup entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 06416, hot and sour soup) and HPB Singapore Foodo Chinese soup category data. The exact Din Tai Fung recipe is proprietary and not available in any approved primary source. 2. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant regular bowl; actual weight may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy hot and sour soup entry and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this soup category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a restaurant-prepared soup with no hydrogenated fats in the ingredient list. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is listed as false because Din Tai Fung Singapore is not certified halal. 7. The romanized_local field uses Mandarin Pinyin romanization (Là wèi suān là tāng). 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03311069 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are used as closest parent matches; neither code precisely identifies the spicy variant of this dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Built complete record from scratch. Adapted nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 06416 and HPB Singapore Foodo. Populated all mandatory multilingual fields for Singaporean Chinese dish (English, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Estimated GI from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency. Set trans_fat_g to 0.0 with confirmed zero justification in notes. Ontology codes set as closest_parent with gap notes. All hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria satisfied on first iteration.

Iteration 2: Corrected Q8 failure by adding the missing romanized alias 'La wei suan la tang' from aliases_by_language.romanized to the flat aliases list, ensuring perfect consistency between the two fields as required by the Aliases consistency rule (CRITICAL — q8).
